Author: Armin Windlin
Title: I Built a 24-Tool Web App in a Few Hours Using Agentic Engineering — Here’s How
Text:
I Built a 24-Tool Web App in a Few Hours Using Agentic Engineering — Here’s How
This week I set out to build Tool Atlas — a privacy-first collection of browser-based utilities from scratch. By the end of a single session, I had 24 fully functional tools, each with internationalization in 5 languages, end-to-end tests, SEO scaffolding, and a polished responsive UI.
No team. No sprints. Just me and an AI coding agent.
This is a story about agentic engineering — a way of building software that I believe will define the next decade of product development.
What Is Agentic Engineering?
Traditional AI-assisted coding is what most developers have experienced by now: autocomplete on steroids. You type a function signature, the AI suggests the body. Useful, but incremental.
Agentic engineering is fundamentally different. Instead of completing your lines, the AI agent operates as an autonomous collaborator. You describe what you want at a high level — “add a bill splitter tool to the utility category” — and the agent:
- Creates the Nuxt component with full business logic
- Adds i18n translation keys in all 5 supported languages (English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese)
- Registers the tool in the central registry
- Maps it into the routing system
- Updates unit test expectations
- Writes end-to-end Playwright tests
- Runs the entire CI pipeline (lint, typecheck, unit tests, e2e tests, SEO checks, performance budgets)
- Fixes any failures it finds
- Verifies the tool in a real browser
That’s not autocomplete. That’s a developer who never sleeps, never forgets a step, and runs the full test suite after every change.
The Technical Stack
- Nuxt 4 + Vue 3 Server-side rendered for SEO, hydrated for interactivity
- Tailwind CSS Utility-first styling with responsive design baked in
- TypeScript End-to-end type safety
- Playwright 73 end-to-end tests covering every tool
- i18n 5 languages with locale-prefixed URLs and hreflang tags
- SEO Dynamic meta tags, JSON-LD structured data, sitemap, robots.txt
The architecture is registry-driven: every tool is declared in a central registry file, and the dynamic route system, sitemap generator, and SEO metadata all read from it. Adding a new tool means adding one registry entry and one Vue component — the infrastructure handles the rest.
Try It Yourself
Tool Atlas is live and free to use. Every tool runs in your browser with zero data collection. Here are some starting points:
- QR Code Generator Generate QR codes instantly from any text or URL
- Base64 Encode/Decode Encode and decode Base64 strings in your browser
- Meeting Cost Calculator See what your meetings really cost
- Bill Splitter Split restaurant bills with tip presets
- Water Intake Calculator Get a daily water recommendation based on your weight
What’s Next: 500 Tools and Beyond
24 tools is just the starting point. The goal for Tool Atlas is to grow to 500+ tools — covering everything from developer utilities and design helpers to finance calculators, education tools, and niche converters most people didn’t know they needed.
The registry-driven architecture was designed for exactly this kind of scale. Adding a new tool doesn’t touch routing, sitemap generation, or SEO infrastructure — it’s one registry entry and one component. With the agentic workflow dialed in, I can realistically add 10–20 new tools per session. The bottleneck isn’t engineering anymore; it’s deciding which tools are most useful to build next.
If there’s a browser tool you wish existed, let me know— it might be live by tomorrow.
The era of spending months building applications is ending. Agentic engineering doesn’t replace developers — it removes the parts of development that were never the hardest part to begin with. The hard part was always deciding what to build and how to build and orchestrate it. Now you can find out if your idea works before lunch.
